## Consensus chooses one history from many valid candidates

By Lecture 4, students know how transactions can be validated and how blocks can be
hash linked. That still does not solve the core decentralized problem: which history
should everyone accept if different nodes see different candidate blocks?

Consensus is the rule set that lets a decentralized network converge on one accepted
ledger history without handing control to a central bookkeeper.

## Why transaction validity is not enough

Two conflicting transactions can both look locally plausible before the network has
settled on one shared ordering.

That is the double-spending problem in decentralized form:

- one user may try to spend the same funds twice,
- two miners may produce competing blocks at nearly the same time,
- different nodes may temporarily disagree about which tip is current.

Consensus is what resolves those conflicts. It turns a set of individually valid
objects into one accepted sequence of state changes.

## What a canonical chain rule does

A blockchain network needs a public rule for selecting between competing histories.
In Bitcoin-like systems, this is usually described as following the chain with the
most cumulative proof of work.

Students often hear "the longest chain," but the deeper point is cumulative work, not
just raw block count. The network wants a rule that can be checked by everyone and
that makes history expensive to rewrite.

This gives a practical answer to forks:

- if two candidate tips appear, nodes may temporarily disagree,
- later blocks add more evidence in favor of one branch,
- and the weaker branch is abandoned as a stale fork.

## Why consensus sits after block structure

Consensus does not replace cryptography, transaction validation, or block structure.
It depends on them.

- Hashes make history tamper evident.
- Signatures and transaction rules define valid state transitions.
- Block structure defines what candidate ledger history looks like.
- Consensus decides which candidate history becomes authoritative.

That ordering matters pedagogically. Students should see consensus as a selection
mechanism layered on top of already valid candidate blocks.

## What students should be able to explain

After this concept, students should be able to explain:

- why a decentralized ledger needs a public chain-selection rule,
- how temporary forks arise even when nodes follow the protocol,
- why consensus solves a different problem from transaction validation,
- and why the network converges on one canonical history rather than many.
